course_title: The World of Classical Athens: Politics and Society

learning_outcomes.description: Understand salient features of the society, culture and political institutions of classical Athens
Explain the operation and history of the most fully developed democracy of pre-modern times
Employ different types of evidence for historical research
Collect and analyse appropriate data in order to answer set questions
Communicate the results of independent research in a written format which conforms with the conventions of Ancient History
Discuss important controversies and areas of intense research interest in contemporary scholarship on classical Athens
Undertake third-year capstone courses in Ancient History.

assessments.task_description:
Weighting: 15%

Every student is required to prepare (read, take notes, etc.) the ancient source or sources and the required readings that are set for each tutorial and to contribute actively to the general discussions of the course. There is no requirement to do an in-class presentation. Participation in tutorials is expected and you are strongly encouraged to attend and participate in the lectures. The student must participate in at least one tutorial to not receive a 0 for this piece of assessment.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date: The Tutorial Paper is due 1 week after the tutorial in which the paper’s topic was discussed at or before 2pm.
assessments.task_description:
Weighting: 20%

Format: Essay

Word length: 1000 words.

Due date: 1 week after the tutorial in which the paper’s topic was discussed at or before 2pm.

Submission: The tutorial paper must be uploaded on Blackboard.

Every student must submit a 1000-word essay, which answers the essay question of a tutorial. Your answer should be based on the set primary source or sources of the tutorial, recommended pieces of modern scholarship in the Course Summary, and any other relevant ancient sources and scholarship you come across in your own research. Failure to include any ancient sources or modern scholarship will result in a 0 for this assessment.

This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools.

A word count that is within ±10% of the set length (word limit) is acceptable. Word count does not include footnotes or bibliography; however, discursive footnotes are included in the word count. A word count that is outside these 10% will receive a proportionate penalty and will be graded against the grading criteria.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date: The In-Semester Exam will take place during the lecture of week 8 (Thursday 17 September) and in the same room.
assessments.task_description:
Weighting: 25%

Duration: 90 minutes + 10 minutes reading time.

Format: The exam will consist of short answer questions, a source analysis, and an essay.

Due date: The In-Semester Exam will take place during the lecture of week 8 (Thursday 17 September) and in the same room. Check if you have any overlapping classes and ensure you can attend the exam.

Beforehand, every student will have to prepare (read, take notes, etc.) two articles/book chapters which respectively have been written by a scholar who takes up a different position in a scholarly debate concerning classical Athens. These articles/book chapters will be made available on Blackboard at the beginning of the semester. The student is allowed to take one physical copy of these articles/book chapters with them into the examination room, and these may contain personal notes, comments, highlighting, etc.

The In-Semester Exam consists of three distinct parts. In the first part, the student must answer a number of questions concerning the two prepared articles. In the second part, the student must read a number of short excerpts of articles/book chapters/books and argue which of the two prepared articles/book chapters it agrees with more. (E.g., excerpt A more closely aligns with the interpretation provided by scholar of the first prepared article, for the following reasons). In the third part, the student will have to perform a source analysis and take up a position in the scholarly debate themselves. Based on the provided sources, the student must argue whether they agree (more) with one or the other scholar who wrote one of the prepared articles. The student may also argue that they agree with neither scholar and take up a separate position altogether.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
Weighting: 40%

Duration: 120 minutes + 10 minutes reading time.

Format: The exam will consist of short answer questions, a source analysis, and an essay.

Due date: University Exam period, date and start time will be determined by UQ examinations. The Final Exam will be in person. 

This exam consists of three distinct parts. The first part tests the student's factual knowledge of classical Athens. The second part consists of a source analysis. The student must answer question(s) based on the provided source(s). In the third part, the student must write an essay on one of the provided statements.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.
